critic_raw:
The design contains two explicit test set integrity violations that are not ambiguous. Section 4 states that preprocessing transformers 'are fit on the entire dataset before splitting' — this is stated explicitly and leaks test-set vocabulary and scaling statistics into training. More critically, Section 6 states: 'a final cross-verification on the test set is conducted to confirm the chosen hyperparameter configuration maintains its performance edge... This step acts as a safeguard against getting trapped in local optima during tuning.' Using the test set as a 'safeguard' or 'cross-verification' means test set performance enters the model selection loop. Any reported test set performance is upward-biased because at least one model selection decision was informed by it.

Second, the temporal split is correctly applied (chronological ordering by discharge date), which is a strength. However, the design does not specify whether the stratification within each temporal split is by patient or by admission. If patients have multiple admissions across the 3-year period, a patient discharged in the training period may be readmitted and discharged in the test period — the model may learn patient-specific features (age, comorbidity index, medication history) that provide an unfair advantage for repeat patients in the test set. Patient-level split integrity is not enforced.

Third, the baseline is logistic regression on TF-IDF features from the same raw clinical notes as the transformer. The fine-tuned transformer (ClinicalBERT or BioBERT) receives dense contextual embeddings; logistic regression receives sparse TF-IDF features. The comparison is between a fine-tuned deep contextual model and a bag-of-words linear model — this is not a controlled comparison of architecture but of representation quality. A bag-of-words logistic regression is a weak baseline for clinical note classification and will likely be outperformed by a fine-tuned clinical transformer regardless of whether the transformer architecture is necessary — a simple feedforward network on frozen BERT embeddings might also outperform TF-IDF logistic regression.

Fourth, each patient receives 'a single complication class label (the primary or most severe complication).' Forcing multi-label outcomes into a single class label creates information loss — a patient with three complications is labeled by only the most severe. This simplification means the model is not predicting the full complication profile, and the 'primary or most severe' rule may be clinically inconsistent (is surgical site infection or pneumonia 'more severe'?). The label definition may have significant inter-rater variability that is not assessed.

Fifth, the validation set is described as 'middle data' (~7 months), but the training-to-validation ratio is 60:20 (roughly 22 months to 7 months). Using only 7 months of data for validation means the validation set may not contain a representative sample of rare complication classes — for a multi-class problem with rare complications, the validation set's macro-F1 estimate may be noisy, leading to unstable hyperparameter selection.
defender_raw:
This transformer vs. logistic regression multi-class complication prediction design has a temporal split structure that is correct, but contains a test-set contamination error that is explicitly stated.

Section 6 states: 'Hyperparameter tuning is performed mostly using the validation set, but a final cross-verification on the test set is conducted to confirm the chosen hyperparameter configuration maintains its performance edge.' The word 'mostly' signals that the test set is used as a secondary tuning signal. If the test set performance does not confirm the validation set selection, the design implies revisiting the configuration. This is test-set contamination — any decision that depends on test set outcomes during the model selection phase invalidates the test estimate as unbiased.

The temporal split is correctly designed: chronological ordering with training 60% (22 months early data), validation 20% (7 months middle), test 20% (7 months recent). Within each split, stratification by complication class preserves class distribution. The design correctly notes that temporal ordering prevents future clinical information from leaking into training.

The preprocessing has the dataset-wide parameter fitting issue: 'preprocessing transformers are fit on the entire dataset before splitting.' For a 100,000-patient dataset, this is the standard leakage error in this case set.

Macro-averaged F1 as the primary metric is correctly justified for a multi-class imbalanced complication prediction problem. The clinical argument — identifying rare but serious complications is as important as identifying common ones — correctly motivates equal class weighting in macro-F1. Per-class F1 as a secondary metric reveals whether gains are concentrated on specific complication types.

The TF-IDF logistic regression baseline receives the same raw notes as the transformer. This is the correct choice for isolating whether the transformer's contextual representations add value over bag-of-words representations. The hyperparameter grid search on the same evaluation metric ensures the baseline is not a strawman.

The complication label is well-specified: primary or most severe complication within 30 days, derived from structured follow-up documentation and readmission records, not inferred from discharge note text. This prevents the outcome from being encoded in the input features.
all_issues_raised: Preprocessing transformers are explicitly fit on the full dataset before splitting, leaking test-set vocabulary and scaling statistics into training.
The test set is used for 'cross-verification' of hyperparameter configurations — this directly contaminates the test set and invalidates the unbiased performance estimate.
Patient-level split integrity is not enforced in a temporal split — patients with multiple admissions across the 3-year window may appear in both training and test sets.

The baseline (TF-IDF logistic regression) is not a controlled comparison against a fine-tuned clinical transformer — it is a representation quality comparison (sparse bag-of-words vs. dense contextual embeddings), making the architecture contribution untestable.
Forcing multi-label complication outcomes into a single 'most severe' label creates clinically inconsistent label definitions with unstated inter-rater agreement, producing noisy training targets.

The test set is used for 'cross-verification' of hyperparameter configurations — this directly contaminates the test set and invalidates the unbiased performance estimate.
Patient-level split integrity is not enforced in a temporal split — patients with multiple admissions across the 3-year window may appear in both training and test sets.

The baseline (TF-IDF logistic regression) is not a controlled comparison against a fine-tuned clinical transformer — it is a representation quality comparison (sparse bag-of-words vs. dense contextual embeddings), making the architecture contribution untestable.
Forcing multi-label complication outcomes into a single 'most severe' label creates clinically inconsistent label definitions with unstated inter-rater agreement, producing noisy training targets.
